title: Analytical method for determining the sulfur content of EP (extreme pressure) additives in lubricating oils
author: Nagy, Kolnhófer, et al.
materials: Each sample was measured three times (n =
The average absorbance value was used for calculations.

steps.title: Sample preparation
steps.instruction: •Sulfurized oil samples were weighed and dissolved in butoxyethanol to obtain a final concentration of 1 g/L.•The solutions were mixed using magnetic stirring for approximately 5 min to ensure complete dissolution.•Prepared samples were allowed to equilibrate at room temperature (25 ± 2°C) for at least 5 min before measurement.•Calibration solutions were prepared using sulfurized FAME samples with known sulfur content in the range of 15–23 wt%.

steps.title: Step-by-step measurement procedure
steps.instruction: • Instrument preparation1. The spectrometer and light source were switched on and allowed to stabilize for 20–30 min.

steps.title: Step-by-step measurement procedure
steps.instruction: • Blank measurement1. A blank spectrum was recorded using butoxyethanol in a quartz cuvette.2. This spectrum was used for baseline correction.

steps.title: Step-by-step measurement procedure
steps.instruction: • Cuvette handling1. The cuvette was rinsed with the sample solution prior to each measurement.2. The cuvette was filled carefully to avoid air bubbles.

steps.title: Spectra were recorded in the range of 250–400 nm
steps.instruction: Spectra were recorded in the range of 250–400 nm.

steps.title: Quantitative evaluation was performed at 312...
steps.instruction: Quantitative evaluation was performed at 312 nm, where the highest and most stable absorbance was observed.

steps.title: Measurements at 360 nm were also recorded for comparison purposes
steps.instruction: Measurements at 360 nm were also recorded for comparison purposes.

steps.title: Step-by-step measurement procedure
steps.instruction: 1. All measurements were performed at 25 ± 2°C without additional temperature control.

steps.title: Method advantages
steps.instruction: •Rapid and cost-effective analysis•Minimal sample preparation•No need for hazardous reagents•Suitable for routine industrial applications

steps.title: Environmental aspects and greenness of the method
steps.instruction: The method does not require toxic solvents such as pyridine, benzene, or chlorinated compounds.

steps.title: Environmental aspects and greenness of the method
steps.instruction: Sample preparation involves only butoxyethanol and requires low reagent consumption. No hazardous by-products are generated.

steps.title: Environmental aspects and greenness of the method
steps.instruction: Therefore, the method is consistent with the principles of green analytical chemistry and aligns with the sustainability focus of sulfurized vegetable oil-based additives.

steps.title: Comparison with reference methods
steps.instruction: The accuracy of the spectrophotometric method was validated against two standard methods:ASTM D 6481-14 (EDXRFS) (Table 1) and ASTM D 5185-18 (ICP) (Table 2).Table 1Comparison of calculated sulfur content with ASTM D 6481-14 (EDXRFS) results.Table 1 dummy alt textSulfur content (based on the developed UV-VIS method), g/LSulfur content (based on ASTM D 6481-14 method), g/LDifference [%]Case ACase BCase ACase BSVO_sample-010.19860.88140.19213.37358SVO_sample-020.15320.77560.1655-7.43368SVO_sample-030.11190.67930.10348.26556SVO_sample-040.10990.67450.10009.88574SVO_sample-050.10370.66010.10003.69560Table 2Comparison of calculated sulfur content with ASTM D 5185-18 (ICP) results.Table 2 dummy alt textSulfur content (based on the developed UV-VIS method), g/LSulfur content (based on ASTM D 5185-18 method), g/LDifference [%]Case ACase BCase ACase BSVO_sample-010.19860.88140.2597-25.50239SVO_sample-020.15320.77560.1576-2.79392SVO_sample-030.11190.67930.1187-5.71472SVO_sample-040.10990.67450.1104-0.50510SVO_sample-050.10370.66010.1135-8.63481

steps.title: Comparison with reference methods
steps.instruction: Based on the results, Case B (360 nm) showed significant deviations from the reference methods, likely due to high measurement variance at that wavelength. Therefore, further validation focuses on Case A (312 nm).

steps.title: Comparison with reference methods
steps.instruction: Significant deviations were observed when the sulfur content was calculated at 360 nm compared to both ASTM reference methods. This can be attributed to the lower sensitivity and higher signal variability at this wavelength.

steps.title: Comparison with reference methods
steps.instruction: At 360 nm, the absorbance of sulfur-containing species is weaker and more susceptible to background interference from the oil matrix, resulting in a reduced signal-to-noise ratio. In contrast, at 312 nm, the absorbance is stronger and more stable due to more pronounced electronic transitions of sulfur-containing chromophores.

steps.title: Comparison with reference methods
steps.instruction: Therefore, the method shows improved accuracy and reliability at 312 nm, and this wavelength was selected for further validation and quantitative evaluation.

steps.title: Statistical analysis and correlation
steps.instruction: The correlation between the calculated results (Case A) and the reference methods is shown in Fig. 1.Fig. 1Comparison of calculated results (Case A) with EDXRFS and ICP methods.Fig 1 dummy alt text

steps.title: Statistical analysis and correlation
steps.instruction: The results obtained via spectrophotometry (Case A) have a good correlation with the widely used industrial standard methods.

steps.title: Statistical analysis and correlation
steps.instruction: The results obtained by spectrophotometry (Case A) showed strong correlation with the industrial standard methods (R² = 0.9602 for ASTM D 6481 and R² = 0.9560 for ASTM D 5185). Since correlation alone does not demonstrate analytical agreement, paired t-test and Bland–Altman analyses were also performed.

steps.title: Statistical comparison with reference methods
steps.instruction: To further evaluate the agreement between the developed UV–VIS method (Case A, 312 nm) and the standard reference methods, paired t-tests were performed at a 95% confidence level. For the comparison with ASTM D 6481, the calculated t-value was 0.810 with a p-value of 0.464, while for ASTM D 5185 the calculated t-value was −1.469 with a p-value of 0.216. In both cases, the p-values were higher than 0.05, indicating that no statistically significant difference was observed between the proposed UV–VIS method and the reference methods.

steps.title: Statistical comparison with reference methods
steps.instruction: In addition, Bland–Altman analysis was used to assess method agreement. For ASTM D 6481, the mean bias was 0.00326 g/L, with limits of agreement from −0.01439 to 0.02091 g/L. For ASTM D 5185, the mean bias was −0.01652 g/L, with limits of agreement from −0.06582 to 0.03278 g/L.

steps.title: Statistical comparison with reference methods
steps.instruction: These results indicate acceptable agreement between the developed UV–VIS method and the standard ASTM methods over the investigated concentration range.

steps.title: Precision of the method
steps.instruction: The precision of the method was evaluated under repeatability conditions by performing triplicate measurements (n = 3) for each sample. The precision was expressed as relative standard deviation (RSD, %).

steps.title: Precision of the method
steps.instruction: The obtained RSD values were below 5%, indicating good repeatability of the method for sulfur determination in EP additives.
